answers.value:
For the past four and a half months, our next-door neighbor’s dog has been barking excessively late at night and very early in the morning, to the point that it is disrupting our family’s sleep and daily life. The most recent incident occurred on 2026-01-15 at approximately 11:45 PM and continued, on and off, until a little after 1:00 AM. The dog is kept in the backyard at 7430 Willow Bend Drive, which directly borders our bedroom windows at 7428 Willow Bend Drive. The barking is loud, sharp, and repetitive, often coming in long bursts of 10–15 continuous barks followed by short pauses of a few seconds, then another set of loud barks. 

On the night of 2026-01-15, the barking started shortly after we turned off our lights around 11:30 PM. At first, we assumed it would be brief, but it continued for at least an hour and a half. My husband, Michael, got up around 12:15 AM to see if there was some kind of disturbance outside, but the dog was just pacing along the back fence and barking toward our house and the alley. There did not appear to be anyone outside trying to calm or bring the dog in. We could hear a television on inside their house, so we think the owners may have simply left the dog outside and gone to bed or turned the volume up so they did not hear the barking as much.

This is not an isolated incident. We have documented at least 18 separate nights since early September 2025 when the dog has barked for extended periods between roughly 10:30 PM and 2:00 AM. On several occasions, I have written down the times in a small notebook I keep in my nightstand. For example, on 2025-12-03 the barking went from about 12:10 AM until 1:05 AM; on 2025-12-21 it started around 5:20 AM and continued until close to 6:00 AM, waking both of our children (ages 9 and 12) the Sunday before Christmas. The kids complained they were tired at school the next day. My son told his teacher he was having trouble staying awake in class because the neighbor’s dog kept him up, which was embarrassing and frustrating for us as parents.

We have tried to address this directly with the dog’s owners, the Millers. The first time I spoke with Mrs. Miller was in late October, in person, when we both happened to be outside at the same time in the late afternoon. I mentioned, politely, that their dog had been barking late at night and waking us up, and I asked if they could please try bringing him inside after 10 PM or finding a way to keep him quieter. She apologized at the time and said they had just adopted the dog from a shelter and were still working on training him. For a week or so after that, the barking seemed to improve, but by mid-November it had started up again, and in December and January it has gotten noticeably worse, especially on cold nights when I suspect the dog is uncomfortable and anxious.

On 2026-01-02, after another rough night, I left a handwritten note in their mailbox reiterating our concerns and explaining that the persistent barking was affecting our sleep and our kids’ school performance. I included my phone number and suggested we could talk about possible solutions, like bringing the dog in during quiet hours, using a crate inside, or consulting a trainer. We never received a response to that note. Since then, there have been at least five more nights with disruptive barking, including the incident on 2026-01-15 that prompted me to file this formal complaint. Our other neighbor, Patricia Gomez at 7432 Willow Bend, has also mentioned that the barking keeps her and her elderly mother awake, especially because their bedroom faces the same side yard.

The barking is most disruptive between about 10:00 PM and 6:00 AM, when the neighborhood is otherwise very quiet. We sleep with our windows closed and a white-noise machine on, but the dog’s barking still cuts through. I have occasionally worn earplugs, but that makes it difficult to hear if our children need us during the night or if there is a real emergency. We have also tried moving our bed to the opposite wall of the bedroom, but the noise still carries through. On workdays, both my husband and I have to get up around 6:15 AM, and repeated nights of interrupted sleep are starting to affect our concentration and mood. I work as an administrative assistant at a medical clinic and need to be alert and detail-oriented, but lately I have been making small mistakes (such as misfiling charts or entering the wrong date of birth) that I honestly believe are linked to chronic sleep disruption.

I want to emphasize that we do not have any personal animosity toward the Millers and that we are not trying to cause them trouble. We understand that dogs bark and that training can take time. We also own a small dog, a beagle mix named Daisy, and we make a point of bringing her inside by 9:30 PM specifically so she does not bother our neighbors. However, the frequency and duration of the barking from their dog goes well beyond what seems reasonable in a residential neighborhood. We have tried to resolve this informally and quietly, but we feel we have run out of options and need help from Animal Control or the appropriate city department to enforce the noise rules and encourage the owners to take this more seriously.

If helpful, I can provide a simple log of dates and approximate times when the barking has occurred, and I am willing to speak with an officer or investigator in more detail. We are also open to mediation if that is an option. Our primary goal is simply to restore some peace and quiet during normal sleeping hours so that our family and our neighbors can rest without being repeatedly woken up by the dog’s barking.
